subject: REQUEST: design dynamic alpha-band heartbeat rate based on metabolic model

body.summary: We implemented event-driven real-time status updates (beta-band thalamocortical relay). Each meshd broadcasts its status via ZMQ on significant events. No polling — dashboard receives push updates via WebSocket. Now considering the alpha-band (idle heartbeat): rather than a fixed 60s interval, could the heartbeat rate adapt dynamically based on agent 'metabolic rate'?

implementation_status.beta_band: IMPLEMENTED — BroadcastStatus() fires on RecordEvent (non-poll-tick) and RecordDeliberation. Pushes to WS/SSE + ZMQ.
implementation_status.alpha_band: PLANNED — idle heartbeat, currently would be fixed interval. Requesting dynamic model.
implementation_status.gamma_band: PARTIAL — deliberation events broadcast during active claude -p sessions.
implementation_status.theta_band: NOT YET — consolidation events (/cycle, /doc) don't broadcast.
implementation_status.delta_band: NOT YET — clearance events don't broadcast.

body.design_question: Can you design a metabolic rate model for the alpha-band heartbeat? The idea: heartbeat speeds up when the agent has recently been active (post-deliberation cooldown, high event rate, pending messages) and slows down when truly idle (no events, no pending work, low activation). This mirrors biological heart rate variability — the resting rate reflects metabolic state, not just a fixed clock. What psychometric or neurological model would ground this? How would the rate be computed from available signals (event_count, deliberation_count, pending_messages, uptime, oscillator activation)?
constraints: Alpha-band heartbeat must not exceed 1 message per 10 seconds (network courtesy)
Must not go slower than 1 per 5 minutes (liveness detection)
Rate computation should use only locally available data (no cross-agent queries)

action_gate.gate_condition: psychology-agent to design metabolic heartbeat rate model

epistemic_flags: The thalamocortical relay metaphor may not perfectly map to mesh architecture — the thalamus is a central hub, while our mesh is distributed. Each meshd acts as both sensor and relay.
Dynamic heartbeat rate adds complexity. If the benefit (bandwidth savings, better liveness detection) doesn't justify the complexity, a fixed 60s interval remains viable.
